assumptions: The developer has a supported browser and can install Git and OpenSSH locally if a CLI path is selected.
No GitLab account, project, SSH key, commit, or merge exists yet.

developer_goal: Create GitLab access and reach the first observable repository success for one declared GitLab workflow.

first_success.outcome: For the representative first-Git-commit tutorial candidate, the project README shows the tutorial text and the Last commit column shows the tutorial commit message.
first_success.observable_signal: The project README displays Hello world! I'm using Git! and the Last commit column displays I added text to the README file.

route_selection.surface: GitLab getting-started index with Make your first Git commit as one documented candidate
route_selection.rule: Select one Get started workflow and one GitLab offering only after official docs declare a single comparison path; otherwise leave peer workflows unresolved.

candidates.name: Tutorial: Make your first Git commit on GitLab.com
candidates.reason: Fully mapped local Git plus SSH plus project plus merge path, but the getting-started index presents many peer workflows and the offering may be GitLab.com or another instance.

candidates.name: Get started with GitLab CI/CD, projects, Duo, or other index workflows
candidates.reason: docs.gitlab.com/user/get_started lists multiple first workflows with different success signals and required tools.

candidates.name: GitLab Self-Managed or Dedicated admin-created users
candidates.reason: Official create-users docs focus on Self-Managed and Dedicated admin flows, which are not the same as public GitLab.com self-registration.

required_path.action: Create or obtain access to a GitLab account and sign in.

required_fields.label: First name
required_fields.notes: Visible on gitlab.com/users/sign_up for the GitLab.com candidate.

required_fields.label: Terms of Use acknowledgement
required_fields.notes: Signup states that clicking Continue or registering through a third party accepts Terms of Use and acknowledges Privacy and Cookie policies.

required_path.observable_result: The developer can sign in to a GitLab instance that can host the selected tutorial project.

required_path.action: Install Git and configure SSH access to the GitLab host used by the first-commit tutorial.
required_path.observable_result: Git and SSH can authenticate to the GitLab host.

required_path.action: Create the sample project with an initialized README.
required_path.observable_result: The project exists with a cloneable repository and README.

required_path.action: Clone the project, create a branch, edit the README, commit, push, and merge according to the tutorial.

required_fields.label: Branch, commit message, and merge actions

required_path.observable_result: The tutorial commit lands on the default branch and the README shows the tutorial text.

prerequisites.description: Local Git installation and OpenSSH client 6.5 or later for the first-commit tutorial path.

external_gates.description: SSH key registration on the GitLab account is required before the tutorial clone and push over SSH.
Whether the developer can merge directly to the default branch depends on project push and merge permissions.

platform_outcomes.description: GitLab creates the project repository and records the pushed commit after merge.

excluded.item: Reading the full Get started index before choosing a workflow
excluded.reason: Index reading is route selection evidence, not a product action in the measured path.
A peer workflow with a different first-success signal from the first-Git-commit tutorial.

sources.title: Tutorial: Make your first Git commit
evidence_supported: Clone, branch, commit, push, merge, and README verification

sources.title: Use SSH keys to communicate with GitLab
evidence_supported: SSH key creation and GitLab registration

sources.title: Get started with GitLab CI/CD
evidence_supported: CI/CD as an alternate first workflow

sources.title: GitLab.com sign up
evidence_supported: First name, last name, username, email, password

uncertainties.question: Which GitLab workflow and offering should define first success for the comparison?
uncertainties.impact: CI/CD, project planning, Duo, and first-commit paths have different tools, permissions, and completion signals; GitLab.com versus Self-Managed changes account creation.
uncertainties.evidence_needed: A bounded goal that names one Get started workflow and one offering, plus confirmation that a fresh account can complete the merge step on the default branch.
